text.title: Pain Point Scoring and Categorization
text.summary: Eight pain categories, the 5-factor weighted scoring matrix, priority thresholds, and antipatterns in pain scoring.

list.item: Time Waste — takes too long to do X (example: "spend 2 hours updating reports").
Money Loss — costs more than it should (example: "paying $200/mo for features I don't use").
Complexity — too hard to understand or configure.
Integration — tools don't work together; copy-paste between apps.
Reliability — doesn't work consistently; crashes regularly.
Support — can't get help; no response from support.
Process — workflow is broken; too many approvals for simple changes.
Learning — hard to get started; long setup time.

rule.statement: Score every pain point using: Score = (Frequency x 0.30) + (Severity x 0.25) + (Reach x 0.20) + (Spend x 0.15) + (Alternatives x 0.10). Scale each factor 1-5.
rule.rationale: The weighted formula prevents single-factor inflation. Frequency dominates because a pain that affects users daily is a stronger product anchor than a catastrophic but rare pain.

list.item: Reach (20%): 1 = few people, 3 = many, 5 = everyone in defined audience.
Spend (15%): 1 = $0 spent solving it, 3 = some spend, 5 = significant spend.
Alternatives (10%): 1 = many alternatives exist, 3 = few, 5 = none.

rule.statement: Priority thresholds: score above 4 = critical (high priority), 3-4 = significant (worth solving), 2-3 = moderate (could be a feature), below 2 = minor (deprioritize).
rule.rationale: Clear numeric thresholds prevent subjective override of the scoring. A score of 3.8 is not "basically a 4"; it is significant but not critical.

antipattern.description: Inflating Reach to 5 ("everyone has this") without three distinct sources mentioning it.
antipattern.reason: Complaints are loud and unrepresentative. Cap Reach at 4 unless multiple independent sources confirm broad prevalence.

antipattern.description: Splitting scoring across multiple agent calls, each with a fresh context window.
antipattern.reason: The scoring rubric drifts between calls; item 40 gets a different standard than item 1. Score the full batch in one agent call with the rubric in the system prompt.

antipattern.description: Mining only one source type (e.g., only Reddit) and treating results as representative.
antipattern.reason: Reddit skews toward complainers and outliers. The "fine but mediocre" majority — where most opportunity lives — doesn't post there.

text.reference: Python script that reads JSON list of pains, computes weighted scores, outputs CSV
